body_txt: The Big Bad has wronged The Hero in various ways: They manipulated them, betrayed them, tortured all their family and friends in front of them , and may have done even more horrible things, but instead of seeking retribution, the offended party quickly forgives his offender. After all, " To err is human, to forgive is divine ." note Making mistakes is part of human nature, and it's better to forgive than to be vengeful, so long as they essentially have inherent goodness in them. And within a season or maybe even an episode, the two become friends and whatever evil the villain did is swept under the rug and forgotten. However, while this is all good and dandy for the characters, the same couldn't be said for the audience who might be rather baffled that such a despicable person is currently singing Kumbaya with their victim(s) rather than being held accountable for their atrocities. This trope describes a reaction where forgiveness appears too easily granted from the perspective of the audience, especially if the character has committed vile acts beforehand . One reason why this happens is because redeeming a villain deprives the audience of the satisfaction of seeing them get their just desserts . For some, the hope of seeing a despicable person finally get what's coming to them is why they continued to consume the story, and seeing them apologize, be forgiven and get a happy ending after everything they did can feel worse than anything they did while they were still evil. This reaction can also apply to non-villainous or even heroic characters who are sufficiently remorseful of their past actions and/or have made attempts to atone for their misdeeds, but whom the viewers believe still needs to face some sort of consequence for their actions rather than be allowed to go completely off the hook. Naturally, everyone has a different threshold as to what they can and will forgive , what one fan may consider too grave to ever be forgiven , another fan may see it as something that can be amended if the person puts genuine efforts to atone for it. This can cause the forgiven character to become a Base-Breaking Character at best or The Scrappy at worst. Compare Karma Houdini (when the offending character is unrepentant and remains unpunished for their misdeeds), Forgiven, but Not Forgotten (where the hero is willing to forgive, but not if it happens a second time), Go and Sin No More (when granting forgiveness is the punishment, with the assumption that the offender will make amends), Honor Thy Abuser where a victim forgives their abuser and This Is Not My Life to Take (when a victim does not take revenge because they think someone else is more entitled to it). Contrast Easily Condemned , when a character is condemned immediately after making a mistake. Compare and contrast with Karmic Overkill , when fans believe that a character's fate was way harsher than deserved. May overlap with Unintentionally Unsympathetic . May also overlap with Angst? What Angst? if the jerk/villain's victims aren't shown affected by the mistreatment they received in any way whatsoever. Or Designated Hero if the protagonist is easily forgiven for their actions despite them being the main cause of the issues at hand or/and being just as bad if not worse than the actual villains themselves. It can also overlap The Forgiven Offer Forgiveness . When characters genuinely forgive other characters easily, it's usually a case of Turn the Other Cheek . Please keep examples from degrading into Complaining About Shows You Don't Like or bashing those you disagree with. Every redeemed character has at least one person think they should have been punished instead of forgiven. Only add examples if there's an objective metric of people having issues with a character being swiftly forgiven AND if said quick forgiveness is a major talking point the fanbase has about said character(s). A No Recent Examples rule applies to this trope and examples shouldn't be added for 6 months . This is measured from the point when the character was forgiven. Time and perspective are needed to see where consensus (if there is one) lies. Also, No Real Life Examples as forgiveness can be a rather hot button issue in real life.
